scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plato's dialogue is set in Athens and describes naming conventions practiced by Athenian families and citizens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Plato's approximate floruit (-384) when he wrote his middle-period dialogues reflecting contemporary Athenian social norms.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mention of priests and sophists reflects the religious and intellectual marketplace of 4th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The practice of purification after inspiration is contemporary to Plato's writing around -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The ritual priority of Hestia in sacrifices was a standard feature of Greek panhellenic and Athenian civic religion.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plato describes this as an established practice in his own time, dated to -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The eschatological views presented by Socrates reflect Athenian philosophical and Orphic/Pythagorean influences of the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: These theological assertions are contemporary to Plato's authorship in -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The association of Artemis with virginity and 'virtue' reflects the prevailing cultic and moral attributes assigned to her by Classical Greeks.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This etymological and theological interpretation belongs to the 4th-century BCE context of Plato (-384).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Socrates observes linguistic influences on Greeks, particularly those in contact with eastern powers like the Persians or Phrygians.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The 4th-century BCE Athenian perspective on 'barbarian' loanwords is contemporary to Plato's floruit (-384).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes internal Athenian linguistic shifts and the conservative speech patterns attributed to Athenian women.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The observation regarding contemporary dialect changes is dated to Plato's writing in -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The pedagogical sequence described is reflective of the educational curriculum (mousike) available to Athenian citizens in the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The educational norms cited by Socrates were active during Plato's floruit in -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Socrates references the communicative practices of the deaf/mute as an observable social fact in his own city.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This social observation is contemporary to the writing of the dialogue in -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The concept of the 'lawgiver' (nomothetes) as the founder of language is a staple of Greek political philosophy and Athenian civic identity.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Plato's philosophical treatment of the lawgiver reflects 4th-century BCE thought (-384).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The theoretical role of the lawgiver in naming is framed within the Athenian intellectual environment of the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated as a principle of art and law during Plato's floruit in -384.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exhortation to the youth (Cratylus) reflects the Socratic instructional culture prevalent in 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The intellectual duties of young students are contemporary to Plato's time (-384).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian writes in the 2nd century CE during the High Roman Empire, reflecting the social stratification and patronage of Greek-speaking Roman cities.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian's satirical observations on social status and dinner parties are contemporary to his life around 165.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The relationship between 'philosophers' and wealthy patrons like Eucrates is a defining feature of the Second Sophistic in the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the professional pressures of 2nd-century CE intellectual life during Lucian's floruit (165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Sending portions of a feast to absent high-status guests was a common Roman and Greco-Roman social courtesy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This patronage practice was active during Lucian's period in the mid-2nd century CE (165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The authority of the paterfamilias or householder to manage dining space reflects the domestic hierarchy of the Roman era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The social conduct described is contemporary to Lucian's 2nd-century CE context (165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The separation of women into specific quarters (gynaikonitis) remained a conceptual and spatial norm in many Greek/Roman households described by Lucian.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The domestic seclusion of women is treated as a contemporary reality in Lucian's time (165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Roman law allowed for the designation of testamentary heirs, a practice central to the plot of many Roman-era satires.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the legal norms of succession during Lucian's floruit in 165.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The adoption of more prestigious names (cognomina) upon achieving wealth was a common social phenomenon in the Roman Imperial period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The satire targets the status anxiety of Lucian's own 2nd-century CE society (165).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The laws of intestate succession (rules of kin inheritance) described are consistent with Roman legal codes like the SC Tertullianum or earlier civil law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The legal mechanism for relative-based inheritance is contemporary to Lucian's writing in 165.
